interest: Behind this argument sits the interest of equity-oriented actors (civil society, reform advocates, parents) and employers who all want a coherent, whole-system reform whose reach is not undercut by a large opt-out sector where privileged cohorts concentrate.
value: It pits equity and system-wide fairness against institutional autonomy/subsidiarity of non-state maintainers.
fear: The driving fear is a theatrical reform that changes the label but not the machinery—informal SES sorting (interviews, reputation, family networks) simply migrates to or persists in church/foundation schools, leaving the same children excluded under a new name.
affected: families subject to the test-prep arms race
employers seeking a broad, uniformly identified talent pool
assumption: It assumes that non-state maintainers will not voluntarily align with reformed rules and that their retained discretion is substantial enough to absorb or reproduce the SES skew the reform targets.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: whether non-state opt-out actually produces a two-tier system at scale depends on maintainer behaviour and enrolment shares that have not been measured under a reformed regime, and the SES indicator needed to detect the skew does not yet exist.
